rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioners First Set Of Demands For The Production And Inspection Of Documents, First Set Of Special Interrogatories, And First Set Of Form Interrogatories; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Amy Shearer; Declaration Of Tracy Sacca; [Proposed Order] (15-130)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioners First Set Of Demands For The Production And Inspection Of Documents, First Set Of Special Interrogatories, And First Set Of Form Interrogatories. Pro Tem Judge Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is GRANTED. No opposition filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: STEVEN JAY ECHTMAN VS. KOSTOPOULOS LAW GROUP PC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 12, DEFENDANT KOSTOPOULOS LAW GROUP PC, A. RITA KOSTOPOULOS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendants Kostopoulos Law Group P.C. and A. Rita Kostopoulos' motion for summary judgment is granted. It is undisputed that Ms. Kostopoulos did not advise Mr. Echtman to delay filing for bankruptcy protection although she was aware that Mr. Echtman had transferred his severance pay to his girlfriend for "safekeeping" several months before he retained Ms. Kostopoulos's services. It is also undisputed that Ms. Kostopoulos did not timely communicate to her client, Mr. Echtman, the settlement offer by the Trustee's counsel during the bankruptcy proceedings. (Echtman depo. 113-114; Lucey Decl. Exh.3; Echtman Decl. Exh. 14). Nevertheless, Defendants are entitled to summary judgment because the undisputed facts show that: (1) Mr. Echtman achieved a better outcome than he would have, had he instructed Ms. Kostopoulos to accept the original settlement offer (which he did not), which would have imposed a three-year refiling bar rather than the two-year bar that was imposed as a result of the stipulation resolving the adversary proceeding; and (2) since Mr. Echtman filed his bankruptcy petition in September 2014, none of his creditors have taken legal action to collect on Mr. Echtman's debts or have communicated to Mr. Echtman their intention of doing so. (UMF 33-35.) Thus even assuming that Mr. Echtman would have been ineligible to obtain a Chapter 7 bankruptcy after the filing bar was lifted in November 2017, Mr. Echtman has failed to show that he has sustained any "actual loss or damage resulting from the attorney's negligence," which is an essential element of his claim for legal malpractice. (Coscia v. McKenna & Cuneo (2001) 25 Cal.4th 1194, 1199.) "'[D]amages may not be based upon sheer speculation or surmise, and the mere possibility or even probability that damage will result from wrongful conduct does not render it actionable. [Citations.] 'Damage to be subject to a proper award must be such as follows the act complained of as a legal certainty . . . .'" (Ferguson v. Lieff, Cabraser, Heimann & Bernstein (2003) 30 Cal.4th 1037, 1048.) The possibility that Plaintiff's creditors may seek in the future to collect on his past debts is too uncertain to establish that he has sustained damages a cause of action for professional negligence and summary judgment is therefore granted. (See Jordache Enterprises, Inc. v. Brobeck, Phleger & Harrison (1998) 18 Cal.4th 739, 749-750 ["The mere breach of a professional duty, causing only nominal damages, speculative harm, or the threat of future harm-not yet realized-does not suffice to create a cause of action for negligence."]; Philipson & Simon v. Gulsvig (2007) 154 Cal.App.4th 347, 364 ["Generally speaking, to be actionable, harm must constitute something more than nominal damages, speculative harm, or the threat of future harm-not yet realized" (internal quotations omitted)]; Blain v. Doctor's Co. (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1048, 1063 ["There is no cognizable harm attributable to the mere exposure to liability. The exposure must result in compensable detriment."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BEATRIZ BULCAO ET AL VS. FCA US LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Enforce Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFFS BEATRIZ BULCAO, BRITNEY LOPEZ'S Motion To Enforce Settlement Plaintiffs Beatriz A. Bulcao and Britney B. Lopez's motion to enforce settlement is denied. The letter memorializing the parties' settlement (Morse Decl., Ex. A) was not signed by Plaintiffs. (Code Civ. Proc. § 664.6(a) ["If parties to pending litigation stipulate, in a writing signed by the parties outside the presence of the court . . . for settlement of the case, the court, upon motion, may enter judgment pursuant to the terms of the settlement." (Emphasis added.)]; Levy v. Superior Court (1995) 10 Cal.4th 578, 586 ["the term 'parties' as used in section 664.6 . . . means the litigants themselves, and does not include their attorneys of record"]; Harris v. Rudin, Richman & Appel (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 299, 305 [statute's requirement of a writing "signed by the parties" requires "the signatures of the parties seeking to enforce the agreement under section 664.6 and against whom the agreement is sought to be enforced"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DALE PALMER VS. RESTAURANT CHEETAH GROUP, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion To Amend The Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFF DALE PALMER'S Amended Notice Of Motion To Amend The Complaint Plaintiff Dale Palmer's motion to amend the complaint is granted. California's strong policy of liberality in amendment of pleadings supports the motion. (See, e.g., Howard v. County of San Diego (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1422, 1428 ["The policy favoring amendment is so strong that it is a rare case in which denial of leave to amend can be justified."].) Defendants have not shown that Plaintiff unduly delayed in seeking amendment or that they would be prejudiced by allowing the amendment. Defendants may raise any challenge to the adequacy of the amended complaint by way of demurrer, motion to strike, or summary judgment/adjudication. (See Brown v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 824, 843 ["The 'better course of action' is to allow a plaintiff to amend the complaint 'and then let the parties test its legal sufficiency in other appropriate proceedings.'"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JACQUELINE SULLIVAN VS. KATY MOTIELY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Plaintiffs Motion To Vacate Dismissal
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 7 PLAINTIFF JACQUELINE SULLIVAN Motion To Vacate Dismissal Plaintiff Jacqueline Sullivan's motion to vacate dismissal of complaint is granted. Plaintiff has shown that the request for dismissal of the complaint without prejudice was entered as a result of her mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect, and has timely sought relief. (Code Civ. Proc. § 473(b).) Defendant mistakenly argues, relying on Huens v. Tatum (1997) 52 Cal.App.4th 259, that discretionary relief under Section 473 is not available for voluntary dismissals. Huens held only that "the mandatory 'attorney affidavit' provisions of section 473 cannot be used to set aside a voluntary dismissal executed pursuant to a settlement." (Id. at 265; see Zamora v. Clayborn Contracting Group, Inc. (2002) 28 Cal.4th 249, 256 ["the Huens majority confined its analysis to the mandatory relief provision of section 473, subdivision (b). Huens therefore has no bearing on our interpretation of the discretionary relief provision. Indeed, the same Court of Appeal has subsequently limited Huens to the mandatory relief context and applied the discretionary relief provision to a voluntary dismissal entered with the consent of the attorney for the party seeking relief."] (citing State Farm Fire & Casualty Co. v. Pietak (2001) 90 Cal.App.4th 600, 609-610).) Moreover, in Zamora v. Clayborn Contracting Group, Inc. (2002) 28 Cal.4th 249, the Supreme Court disapproved Huens and expressly rejected the argument-identical to Defendants' position here-that Section 473(b) "applies only to involuntary judgments or dismissals." (Id. at 254, 256.) Rather, the Court observed, "California courts have long held that '[e]ven after a voluntary dismissal with prejudice has been filed, the trial court has jurisdiction to vacate the judgment of dismissal under Code of Civil Procedure section 473 where it has been entered as a result of the plaintiff's 'mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect.'" (Id. at 254-255, quoting Basinger v. Rogers & Wells (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 16, 21.) Defendant's other arguments are meritless. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANNA MARTIN VS. PHL VARIABLE INSURANCE COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Phl Variable Insurance Company'S Demurrer To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 13, Defendant Phl Variable Insurance Company'S Demurrer To Complaint [part 1 of 2 for tentative ruling] Defendant PHL Variable Insurance Company's special demurrer and its general demurrer to the first, second, and third causes of action for breach of contract, breach of the covenant of good faith and fair dealing, and negligent misrepresentation stated in Plaintiff Anna Martin's complaint are overruled. Demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored and strictly construed because discovery can provide clarification. (See Lickiss v. Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1125, 1135.) The complaint is not fatally uncertain and places Defendants on notice of the claims asserted against them. (Khoury v. Maly's of California, Inc. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616.) A breach of contract cause of action can be pled by alleging the legal effect of the contract. (McKell v. Washington Mutual, Inc. (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1457, 1489.) The Court denies PHL's request that it take judicial notice of the life insurance policy and application; the former is not even a copy of the original policy issued to Mr. Rimac, but only a "representative policy" purportedly containing the same terms and conditions. (Lensink Decl. 2). Likewise, the Court does not take judicial notice that Plaintiff was a 50% beneficiary under the policy, as suggested by the original application in 2003, because this fact is reasonably subject to dispute. (Compl. 11.) Indeed, the schedule page attached to the attached policy states, "Beneficiary and Owner as stated in the application unless later changed." (RJN, Ex. 1 (emphasis added).) (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. uPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 660 ["'[A] court ruling on a demurrer cannot decide a question that may depend on disputed facts by means of judicial notice.'"].) The breach of contract cause of action is sufficiently pled. Plaintiff has alleged that she was the sole beneficiary under the policy and has standing to assert a breach of contract claim for denial of the benefits. (See Hatchwell v. Blue Shield of California (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 1027, 1034 ["A non-party who is nevertheless entitled to policy benefits, such as an 'insured' person under the terms of the policy or an express beneficiary, has standing only if she is the claimant whose benefits are wrongfully withheld."].) Plaintiff has also pled sufficiently that PHL breached the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in denying her benefits under the policy. (Compl. 33). Ms. Martin has adequately pled in the alternative that she was misled and relied on PHL's misrepresentations that Mr. Rimac's intent to have her designated as the sole beneficiary had been effected in the policy. (Compl. 11.) (Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2016) 6 Cal.App.5th 1207, 1222-1223 [pleading in the alternative allowed].) The negligent misrepresentation cause of action is pled with the requisite specificity. (See West v. JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 780, 793 [holding that mortgage borrower met specificity requirement to plead fraud against creditor; plaintiff was not required to plead identity of author of letter because that information was uniquely within the bank's knowledge].) Plaintiff's reliance on the insurance certificate is sufficiently pled and cannot be determined to be unreasonable as a matter of law on the face of the complaint. (See Alliance Mortgage Co. v. Rothwell (1995) 10 Cal.4th 1226, 1239 ["'Except in the rare case where the undisputed facts leaved no room for a reasonable difference of opinion, the question of whether a plaintiff's reliance is reasonable is a question of fact.'"].) [see part 2 of 2 for entire tentative ruling] =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANNA MARTIN VS. PHL VARIABLE INSURANCE COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Phl Variable Insurance Company'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 13, Defendant PHL Variable Insurance Company'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint Defendant PHL Variable Insurance Company's motion to strike portions of Plaintiff Anna Martin's complaint is denied. Plaintiff's bad faith allegations sufficiently support her prayer for relief for emotional distress and punitive damages. (See Jordan v. Allstate Ins. Co. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 1062, 1079 ["if the insurer denies benefits unreasonably (i.e., without any reasonable basis for such denial, it may be exposed to the full array of tort remedies, including possible punitive damages"]; Frazier v. Metropolitan Life Ins. Co. (1985) 169 Cal.App.3d 90, 101, 108 [affirming award of emotional distress damages for bad faith denial of life insurance benefits].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: JONATHAN BORNSTEIN VS. DANIEL BORNSTEIN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Defendants' Motion To Transfer, Or Alternatively For Stay Of Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 14, DEFENDANTs PERETZ & ASSOCIATES, YOSEF PERETZ, and SHANE HOWARTER's Motion To Transfer, Or Alternatively For Stay Of Proceedings Defendants Peretz & Associates, Yosef Peretz, and Shane Howarter's motion to transfer this case to Alameda County Superior Court is denied without prejudice to its renewal. Defendants' motion under Code of Civil Procedure Section 396b(b) for an award of reasonable expenses and attorney's fees incurred in making the motion is denied. Defendants' alternative motion to stay the action pending the final resolution of the action currently in trial in Alameda County Superior Court, Jonathan Bornstein v. Daniel Bornstein, et al., Case No. RG16-830541, is granted. Unlike a motion to transfer venue on "wrong court" grounds, a change of venue motion based on the ground of convenience of witnesses cannot be made before an answer is filed. (Code Civ. Proc. § 396b(b); Cholakian & Associates v. Superior Court (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 361, 368 ["'The court will not entertain a motion for change of venue on the grounds of convenience of witnesses when the defendant has not filed an answer, for the reason that until the issues are joined the court cannot determine what testimony will be material.'" (quoting Johnson v. Superior Court (1965) 232 Cal.App.2d 212, 214).].) This is the most recently filed of multiple actions involving many of the same parties arising out of the dissolution of a law firm that was owned by two brothers, Plaintiff Jonathan Bornstein and Defendant Daniel Bornstein, as well as the separation of other real estate assets and other matters. All of the previously-filed actions are pending in Alameda County, the situs of extensive litigation between the parties, and have been consolidated and stayed pending the resolution of the case currently in trial. Further, several of the causes of action in the instant complaint challenge certain Defendants' conduct in filing and prosecuting at least one of the stayed and consolidated actions, which may be the subject of further proceedings in Alameda County. Under all of the circumstances, a stay of this action pending the final resolution of the Alameda County action currently in trial and the actions that have been stayed and consolidated with that action is necessary to avoid a multiplicity of judicial proceedings and the risk of inconsistent rulings, and would further judicial efficiency. (See Code Civ. Proc. §§ 128, 526(a)(6); Freiberg v. City of Mission Viejo (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1484, 1489 ["Trial courts generally have the inherent power to stay proceedings in the interests of justice and to promote judicial efficiency."]; Glade v. Glade (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1441, 1455-1456 [stay of action appropriate to avoid "a threat of multiple actions and inconsistent verdicts"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BANK OF AMERICA, N.A. VS. JEFFREY A LANG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Deem Requests For Admissions Admitted
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF BANK OF AMERICA, N.A.'S Motion To Deem Requests For Admissions Admitted. Pro Tem Judge Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Off calendar; dismissal filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TERESA SU ET AL VS. STEPHEN MARK MOSKOWITZ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Stephen Moskowitz, Moskowitz Law Corporation, Moskowitz Llp Demurrer To Complaint (Tenative Rulings Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 10, Defendant Stephen Moskowitz, Moskowitz Law Corporation, Moskowitz Llp Demurrer To Complaint (Tenative Rulings Only Part 2 Of 2) Plaintiffs need not attach the written contracts, but may plead verbatim their legal terms or plead their legal effect. (Compl. 18, 25, 32, 75.) (See Miles v. Deutsche Bank National Trust Co. (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 394, 402 ["a plaintiff may plead the legal effect of the contract rather than its precise language."]; McKell v. Washington Mutual, Inc. (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1457, 1489 ["A written contract may be pleaded either by its terms-set out verbatim in the complaint or a copy of the contract attached to the complaint and incorporated therein by reference-or by its legal effect."].) However, although the complaint alleges that Plaintiffs entered into "several contracts" with Defendants (Compl. 46; see also id. 75 ["numerous written agreements"]), it specifies only one of those written agreements by date and subject. (See id. 25.) Accordingly, the demurrer is sustained with 20 days leave to amend so that Plaintiffs may allege with more particularity the specific written contracts they allege Defendants breached. The complaint sufficiently alleges fraudulent business practices likely to deceive the public to state a claim for violation of Business and Professions Code § 17200 et seq. (Compl. 12, 18, 99, 100, 101.) Paragraph 87 is not subject to being stricken, both because such relief is not available on demurrer and because it properly alleges that Plaintiffs seek restitution and relief available under the applicable provisions of the Code. Finally, the plain language of the Consumer Legal Remedies Act extends to "services," and nothing in the statutory language or any reported authority cited by Defendants specifically excludes legal services from its scope. Further, the Act is to be liberally construed. (Civ. Code § 1760 ["This title shall be liberally construed and applied to promote its underlying purposes, which are to protect consumers against unfair and deceptive business practices and to provide efficient and economical procedures to secure such protection."].) Accordingly, the demurrer as to the eleventh cause of action is overruled. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of tentative ruling part 2 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: TERESA SU ET AL VS. STEPHEN MARK MOSKOWITZ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 20, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT STEPHEN MOSKOWITZ, MOSKOWITZ LAW CORPORATION, MOSKOWITZ LLP DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT (tenative rulings part 1 of 2) Defendants Stephen Moskowitz, Moskowitz Law Corporation and Moskowitz LLP's demurrer to the first, second, ninth and eleventh causes of action of the complaint for professional negligence, breach of fiduciary duty, violation of Business and Professions Code § 17200 et seq., and violation of the Consumer Legal Remedies Act is overruled. The demurrer to the fifth cause of action for breach is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Plaintiffs have sufficiently pled a claim for professional negligence. Defendants improperly attack only some of the factual allegations supporting the malpractice claim. (See Chazen v. Centennial Bank (1998) 61 Cal.App.4th 532, 542 ["[a] demurrer does not lie to a portion of a cause of action."].) Further, Coscia v. McKenna & Cuneo (2001) 25 Cal.4th 1194 and Wiley v. County of San Diego (1998) 19 Cal.4th 532 are entirely inapposite. Those cases hold that "when a former criminal defendant sues his or her attorney for legal malpractice, the former client's actual innocence of the underlying criminal charges is a necessary element of the malpractice cause of action." (Coscia, 25 Cal.4th at 1197.) Under Wiley, the actual innocence requirement applies to claims for "criminal malpractice," which the Supreme Court defined as "'legal malpractice in the course of defending a client accused of crime.'" (Wiley, 19 Cal.4th at 536 n.1.) Here, in contrast, there is no allegation that Plaintiffs were accused or convicted of a crime, only that they were the subjects of an investigation that did not result in an indictment. (Compl. 31.) Defendants' argument that the rule in Coscia and Wiley, requiring proof of actual innocence as a prerequisite to a legal malpractice claim, applies to any investigation of potential criminal charges is unsupported by any authority or by the policy considerations underlying the actual innocence requirement, nearly all of which turn on the premise that the malpractice plaintiff is a "guilty defendant." (See Brooks v. Shemaria (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 434, 442-443 [holding that the actual innocence rule does not apply to a claim that the attorney failed to exercise reasonable competence and diligence in seeking the return of the client's seized property following his conviction]; Bird, Marella, Boxer & Wolpert v. Superior Court (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 419, 432 [holding that the actual innocence requirement does not apply to a fee dispute between a criminal defendant and his former attorney].) The complaint's allegations regarding Defendants' failure to refund unearned fees, failure to respond to Plaintiffs' reasonable requests for information, and Defendants' false and misleading representations to Plaintiffs and resulting damage, among others, are sufficient to support a claim for breach of fiduciary duty. (Compl. 22, 34, 58.) (See, e.g., Charnay v. Cobert (2006) 145 Cal.App.4th 170, 182 [allegations that attorney billed for tasks not performed and billed at an inflated rate adequately pleaded a claim for breach of fiduciary duty], citing Bird, Marella, Boxer & Wolpert v. Superior Court (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 419, 431 [all members of the bar owe clients "fiduciary duty to charge only fair, reasonable and conscionable fees"].) (end of teantive ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)
